slides: Openwork launched from YC P26 as an open source alternative to Claude's enterprise collaboration tool. You deploy it on your own infrastructure. Every conversation, every decision, every artifact stays inside your network.

73% of enterprises using closed AI collaboration tools report anxiety about proprietary data training competitor models. Openwork eliminates that risk entirely because there is no third-party model training on your data.
Internal AI collaboration cuts project timelines by 40-60% at early adopter companies. The shift from messaging about work to doing work alongside AI is the biggest productivity jump since git.

Deployment takes under 2 hours on existing infrastructure. Compare that to the average 28-day enterprise SaaS procurement cycle. Your team could be collaborating with AI before IT finishes the vendor assessment.
The real cost of closed AI tools is not the subscription fee. It is the strategic data you are feeding into someone else's training pipeline, forever, for free.
Check out Openwork if you run engineering teams. Link in comments.
posts.caption: Claude Cowork charges per seat. Openwork charges nothing and you keep your data. The open source enterprise AI stack is forming faster than anyone predicted. Who controls your team's AI collaboration data?

posts.topic: Why 67% of enterprise AI deployments fail before month 4
posts.content:
67% of enterprise AI tools deployed in Q1 2025 were abandoned by Q3.

I tracked this across 40 companies in my network. The reason was never the technology.

It was the data policy.

Most companies realize 90 days in that their proprietary code, customer data, and strategic documents are flowing through a third-party API with terms of service that allow model training. Legal panics. Security panics. The tool gets shut down.

The few AI deployments that survive month 4 share one trait: they run on infrastructure the company controls. Not because open source is ideologically better, but because the compliance team will not sign off on anything else.

This is creating a massive bifurcation. Companies that deployed closed AI tools first now face a costly migration to self-hosted alternatives. Companies that waited are skipping directly to open source deployments that satisfy legal from day one.

The lesson is clear. In enterprise AI, the data policy matters more than the model quality. A slightly worse model you can actually deploy will always beat a superior model your legal team blocks.

How long did your company's last AI tool survive before someone asked about data?

posts.topic: Closed AI vendors are structurally incentivized to become your competitor
posts.content:
What happens when your AI vendor becomes your competitor?

I watched a Series B startup spend $180K annually on an AI coding assistant last year. By December, that same vendor launched a product competing directly with the startup's core offering, trained partly on the startup's usage patterns.

This is not a hypothetical risk. It is the structural incentive of every closed AI company. The more you use their tool, the better their model gets at doing what you do.

Open source AI tools flip this dynamic. When you run tools on your own infrastructure, your usage data makes your deployment smarter, not your vendor stronger. The value compounds inside your organization.

I am seeing CTOs at 12 companies in the past month explicitly banning closed AI tools for core workflows. Not because the tools are bad, but because the data leakage is an existential business risk.

The next generation of enterprise software will not be SaaS. It will be open source deployed behind your own firewall. The vendors who do not understand this will lose their best customers to tools that cost nothing and keep their data.

Would you trust a vendor who could become your next competitor?
